item.title: Laverstock Hill in Bloom
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Laverstock Hill in Bloom

I spent many afternoons during my school years up Laverstock Hill. Although I have not visited in a while, it is still warmly wrapped up in nostalgia, the soft blanket of childhood memory. I have videos and photographs of rolling down the grassy dunes of the field behind my school, of the herd of cows that roved intermittently across the downs, of the swing fashioned from old rope and a plank of wood that sat overlooking the city. In PE, they made us run up and back down the hill for cross country. The yearly ‘mini marathon’ was a mile-round trip of the muddy, trodden paths up to the hill. It has been a while since I’ve done that walk, years, but I can still remember the route like the back of my hand, could do it blindfolded, even. In those memories, I recall a very straightforward time, my life regimented by timetables and lunches and GCSE exams. Yet, they were also the careless years, the years before pretending, when I was old enough to know who I was but still young enough not to question it. Who could hold their handstand for the longest? How do I get out of this year’s Sports Day? Whose house for a sleepover?

These photos are from the last time I walked the route in its entirety, in June of 2020, the peak of Britain’s COVID-19 lockdown. I walked it alone, listened to a few good songs, walked almost directly in the footsteps of myself at fifteen, twelve, seven. It’s still as beautiful as it was then. I start with ‘Wildfire’ by Seafret and end with ‘Getaway’ by Blossoms. These songs too are relics now, almost six years since they were on repeat in all my playlists. I used to have a habit of taking photos of my screen with my Lumix bridge camera, an ‘artsy’ way of flexing my music taste to my 63 followers on Instagram. While writing this, I thought to search Laverstock Hill up online, expecting perhaps a description of the nature or geological structure, but I found nothing. A short article from the Salisbury Journal recounting a similar experience of walking up and viewing the city from the ‘summit’, written in 2010. Laverstock Hill is not a spectacular formation, it is not a noteworthy landscape, and yet to me it has inscribed so many years of my life that it may as well be as important as Ben Nevis or Kilimanjaro. Of course, I jest. But then again, maybe I don’t. We leave ourselves all over the landscape; a place is never just a place.

item.title: Black Swan(s)
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Black Swans

2021, the second year of my undergraduate degree at the University of Exeter. My house is at the very end of the highstreet, almost overlooking the River Exe. If you follow the water, you will reach the quay, bracketed with craft stalls built into the landscape, restaurants and bridges and benches that in their reliable architecture hold (what felt at the time) my entire life and identity. I have recently been gifted my first DSLR, an upgrade from a bridge camera I dutifully and loyally kept safe since I was thirteen years old. I walk up and down the quay every day, often twice a day, itching for company in the tense post-lockdown months, resigned to obsessive work at my rickety wooden desk and these brief, beautiful snatches of sun in the early morning.

Black swans (cygnus atratus) are rare in the UK. In the Merlin app (my trusty ornithology buddy), it is a solid red circle (rare) and sighting one in any given area of the UK is classified as “unlikely”. Black swans are native to southeast and southwest Australia, where they gravitate to both fresh and saltwater lakes, swamps, rivers and wetlands. They are nomadic, with varying migration patterns. In terms of appearance, they are large, black-feathered birds with white flight feathers and vibrant red bills, a bright splash that would draw any wandering eye on an afternoon stroll. Across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, they were introduced as ornamental birds in New Zealand, western Europe and Japan, with limited numbers in the US. Aside from Australia, however, their most prominent geographical association is in Dawlish, Devon. In fact, they are an established emblem of the town [https://ladysmile.co.uk/blogs/black-swans-of-dawlish-the-history-and-influence]. As such, on one routine stroll down the River Exe, I was met with a mating pair (we all deserve a holiday, right?).

After a mad dash back to my house to collect my camera, I spent at least an hour as a desperate paparazzi. Now, they remain the rarest birds (or, I would say, animal in general) that I have witnessed in the wild. I look at these pictures, and I am met not only with the beauty and majesty of their great, streamlined bodies, but a more baseline affection for both Devon and those years of mine in Exeter. I spent a lot of time photographing birds, attracted, I think, by their apparent freedom of movement. But introduction of non-native species within human-led currents is hardly freedom of movement, is it? I stood on the bank of the river and watched these birds and felt a great and affectionate calling. “We are both a little far from home, are we not?”

I had never seen black swans before, and I have not seen them since. I left Exeter and I rarely look back. However, sometimes I am looking through the thousands of photos I have taken over my life and I find these again, and for a moment I can see the crowd of them, the bright flash of red and the distinctive white stripe, and I am back there on the bank, crouching down, close enough to photograph but not to risk provoking them. Two animals facing each other off. One wild and one tame.
